data.title: Progress Software, NASDAQ: PRGS, to release Q3 earnings Sept 29. Forecast: $1.30 EPS, $240.11M revenue - up from $1.26 EPS, $178.69M revenue last year. Stock price: $41.20.
data.description: Progress Software Corporation (NASDAQ: PRGS) is set to announce its third-quarter earnings on Monday, Sept. 29 after the market closes. Analysts are predicting earnings of $1.30 per share, an increase from $1.26 per share in the same period last year. The company, headquartered in Burlington, Massachusetts, is expected to report quarterly revenue of $240.11 million, up from $178.69 million a year ago. Progress Software exceeded earnings expectations in the second quarter. The company's shares closed at $41.20, rising by 0.2% on Thursday. For more information and to access the latest analyst ratings, readers can visit the Analyst Stock Ratings page on Benzinga.

data.title: US gold reserves at 90-year low as world's central banks accumulate gold at a record pace. Global gold holdings (excluding the US) reach 49-year high. US policymakers may need to rethink stance as US now holds only 20% of world's gold reserves, down from over 50%. Central banks worldwide also increasing their gold holdings, reaching a level not seen since 1996. Expert warns of significant shift in global finance.
data.description: U.S. gold reserves have hit a 90-year low, contrasting with central banks worldwide accumulating gold at a high rate. Otavio Costa of Crescat Capital notes this has led to global gold holdings (excluding the U.S.) reaching a 49-year high. The U.S. once held over 50% of global gold reserves, now down to just 20%. Since the end of the gold standard, U.S. reserves as a percentage of total global reserves have declined, while others have increased theirs, especially post-2008 Global Financial Crisis. Foreign central banks hold more gold internationally than in 1996. This shift raises questions on the U.S.'s stance on gold. US gold reserves are now at 90-year lows, whereas the global trend is towards near 50-year highs.

data.title: Buffett Indicator at 216.6%, Shiller CAPE ratio above 40, Powell notes 'fairly highly valued' stocks, sparking bubble speculations.
data.description: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell recently acknowledged the high valuation of equity prices, stating that they are "fairly highly valued". This comes as key valuation metrics have reached levels comparable to those seen during the dot-com crash. The Total Market Cap to GDP ratio, known as the "Buffett Indicator", has risen to 216.6%, well above its historical average. Additionally, the Shiller Cyclically-Adjusted Price-to-Earnings (CAPE) ratio has exceeded 40 for the first time since 2000, nearing its all-time high of 44.19 from December 1999. Analysts are drawing parallels between the current market fervor, driven by AI technology and heavily concentrated in a few large-cap stocks, and past market bubbles. The article further delves into the forward Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio. These indicators collectively suggest that stocks may be overpriced, indicating a potential market bubble.

data.title: Trump's tariffs lead to rising inflation, 72% of CPI components surpass Fed's target. Econ concerns increase.
data.description: Growing concerns about inflation are at the forefront for economists and market analysts, who attribute the current situation to President Donald Trump's trade and tariff strategies. The Kobeissi Letter recently highlighted that an alarming 72% of Consumer Price Index (CPI) components are surpassing the Federal Reserve's target rate of 2%, the highest percentage in three years. This number shows a significant increase from the previous year's 55% and the average of 57% seen in 2018 and 2019. Apollo Global Management's Chief Economist revealed that the surge in CPI components is mainly driven by escalating goods inflation attributed to tariffs. This has led to questions about whether there is a resurgence of inflation overall. Analysts are closely monitoring the situation as inflationary risks continue to rise.
